items.question: What does feature-engineering encoding cover that Encoding Categoricals does not?
items.answer: Encoding Categoricals defines one-hot, ordinal, and target encoding and their pitfalls. This topic covers pipeline composition with ColumnTransformer, cardinality-driven strategy selection, rare bucketing, frequency/hash alternatives, model-aware choices, and serializing encoders with the model for production.

items.question: How do you structure sklearn preprocessing for mixed categorical and numeric columns?
items.answer: Use ColumnTransformer with named branches: OneHotEncoder or OrdinalEncoder on categorical columns, scalers on numerics, optionally passthrough for already-numeric fields. Wrap with the estimator in Pipeline, fit on X_train, and serialize the whole Pipeline for inference.

items.question: When is frequency encoding a reasonable alternative to one-hot?
items.answer: When a nominal column has high cardinality and the model can use a single numeric signal (tree ensembles, regularized linear models)—replace each level with its training-set frequency or proportion, computed on train only, with unseen levels mapped to zero or a documented constant.

items.question: Why must target encoders inside a Pipeline still use cross-validation?
items.answer: Pipeline prevents refitting on test data between steps, but a target encoder that computes in-sample category means still leaks labels during fit. Use encoders or wrappers that compute out-of-fold statistics on the training split—see Encoding Categoricals for the safe target-encoding pattern.
